description: A10FJ - Day 6, part 25: Photos, landmarks and fuel cells

tokens: The vertical stereo photography on the 23rd revolution is from terminator to terminator.
The CSM is in the local vertical attitude at T0as it passes over the spacecraft sunrise terminator as the vertical stereo photography starts.
The CSM is yawed off local vertical as it passes 65° East, T2, to centre the FOV on the approach to and the area around Landing Site 1 which is at 34° East, T3.
Once past Landing Site 1, the CSM will yaw back to local vertical to cover the approach to and area around Landing Site 2.
Once past Landing Site 2, the CSM maintains this attitude and the vertical stereo photography continues to the spacecraft sunset terminator.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford actually means he has Verb 82 running.
The Apolune is displayed in register 1 and the Perilune in register 2.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The vertical stereo photography is to start (T0) at 119:41:31 as read up on the latest photography PAD.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young is referring to mission preparation activity between lunar landing missions.
He is concerned that if the period between missions is extended to 6 months, they will still end up rushing the mission preparation into the last 2 months of that period.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford is calling out the spacecraft sunrise time at 119:32:44.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Basin XV is situated 160 degrees West and is today known as Korolev, named after the engineer who was the 'Chief Designer' of the Soviet space programme.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan is still annoyed at the failure of the Hasselblad camera used in the LM.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Crater 225 is today known as Coriolis.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Crater 303 is today known as Vening Meinesz.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Basin IX, today known as Mendeleev.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Verb 82 is called to display the orbital parameters.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The maps being carried onboard are based on Lunar Orbiter photography, some of which was taken from fairly high altitude.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 120 hours, 7 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Apollo 10 is within some 21 seconds of being acquired as it comes around to the visible face of the Moon on lunar revolution number 23.
Standing by for the first contact and they have the strip photography probably underway at this time.
They may be rather busy and will not have a whole lot to say.
As soon as we do have acquisition and conversation does begin we'll come up with that.
One hour and 11 minutes in this pass until Loss Of Signal.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Presented here are the 58 images taken on magazine R for this vertical and near vertical stereo photography.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Spacecraft communicator Jack Lousma has called the crew, said he's standing by.
We've had no response yet from the crew.
We'll continue to leave the air-ground circuit live for any possible conversation during this revolution.
Several items of information are scheduled to be passed to the crew during this rev but at the present time they're likely quite busy in conducting the photography task from terminator to terminator.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The spacecraft is yawed 20° right (North) to include LS-1 in the strip photography.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We'll open the circuit and let it run until conversation commences even though there's a great deal of background noise, so that no conversation will be missed.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: This is Apollo Control.Snoopy, the ascent stage of the Lunar Module, is now some 36,875 nautical miles [68,292 km] outbound from the Moon, going toward solar orbit, traveling at some 5,356 feet per second [1,633 m/s].
Flight Controllers who have been monitoring the Lunar Module are packing up at this time.
As a matter of fact, the console is vacant now.
They are abandoning the ship as their job is complete.
Background noise has dropped somewhat in the air-ground circuit.
We'll come back live with that now to pick up conversation during this 23rd lunar revolution.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're taking our vertical stereo photography now.
We just rolled past the subsolar point there.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Now that we've got some time to pick out - really concentrate on this stuff, we're finding all kinds of features in here.
And it's mostly been on the tape, but I hope you'll be able to get the tape and play it there.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: While we're talking about the tape, we've been getting on the playback come weak voice and background noise, and we found out on a prior flight that this comes out a lot better if you make sure you have the mike real close to your mouth when you talk into the tape recorder.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: All of you who were talking to the tape recorder.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Having now passed over LS-1, the spacecraft is yawed left 20° to place the camera axis back on the spacecraft groundtrack and to view LS-2.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: While we are waiting for the crew to complete the strip photography session, we have one local note of interest for newsmen here covering Apollo 10.
Ray Zedekar of Flight Crew Support Division will cover the lunar exploration time lines for the Apollo 11 mission, scheduled now for July.
This will be in the main auditorium at the Manned Spacecraft Center.
We return now to monitoring the air-to-ground loop.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Some 5 minutes remaining in this vertical stereo sequence.
The cut-off time at which they reach 34° East lunar longitude will be 120 hours, 30 minutes, 18 seconds; a little less than 5 minutes from now.
Likely there will not be too much conversation until after this stereo task is completed.
Still monitoring air to ground live.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We've just passed over Mount Marilyn and the crater Weatherford.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Additional photos are being taken on magazine M, like this of Landing Site 1.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Yawing right 20 degrees to pick up Landing Site 2, now.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And we're right on top of Maskelyne at this time.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We're just past Maskelyne B, and I'm right - looking straight down at Sidewinder Rille, coming up to the head of Diamondback Rille.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Sabine E is on the left.
On my left as we go backwards.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We observe you're liable to get sunlight on the windows here pretty soon.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We've got Moltke out my right window.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Landing Site 2 is now officially known as Statio Tranquillitatis.
It is the place where Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin landed the Apollo 11 Lunar Module Eagle onto the lunar surface.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And Langrenus is quite a majestic crater with a tremendously beautiful central peak.
One that we were just able to look at sunset the other day that's just beautiful today.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And you'll have to speak up a little louder, please.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: And I see crater 133 with that little crater we talked about on the right of it for tracking.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We have a beautiful panorama looking back from Sabine and Ritter over the landing site back to Maskelyne A and B and then over past Mount Marilyn there.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I'll tell you, later on today - We'll talk about it when we have a chance for a rev, we may just go to a vertical strip, roll 90 degrees so you can get High Gain, and we'll shoot the tube on it, because I know it will pick it up and you can pick out all these features.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Also, you might tell Jack that we couldn't have a better crater named after him, because we are looking at him now back from Sabine and Ritter, and the boulders that have been kicked out of it on the outside slope nearly look like a forest of pine trees, there's so many big black boulders there.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Say again the name of that, Tom.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: It's really spotted the countryside with them.
That's what we code named it Herr Schmidt.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: He says thanks, but it's - spelled the name wrong.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Well, we were in a hurry anyway.
We didn't have too much time to worry about details.
It looks just like a scattered - about the same density, you know, as pine trees up on a mountain ridge.
That's about what these big black boulders look like.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We're now in an area that is really noticeably marked by volcanic activities.
We have all types of lumps - of lumps here and we can really see there's just thousands of volcanoes here, just a tremendous volcanic field at this time.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I've got one interesting site here.
Looks like a - It's probably too dark to take a picture.
but you can see where you've had a big impact crater with the ramped edge.
It looks like a stream of volcanic material has run over into it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: If you went to 1/125 on the Hasselblad.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We've got a lot of Sun on the windows.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Sure hope we got you some good data.
We got it in the sequence camera at one frame per second, color all the way through, and we got the Hasselblad all the way, too.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Judging by the lighting in comparison to the above photos, the sequence camera footage appears to have been shot on 16mm magazine C.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: If you're going to fly the next vertical strip photography, namely in Rev-31, in that same attitude, why, then we can give you the TV.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Did you have High Gain lock on us that time?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We had it most of the way, and if we can confirm that you will fly in the same attitude in Rev-31, why, TV will come through all right.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We will fly the same one as the last.
You get a tremendous panoramic view looking back over Landing Site 1, Over.
[Garble] as you come over Landing Site 2, looking back over the maria area.
We'll try to show it to you.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We'll plan on it for Rev-31, then.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: And the Sun went down and we have the solar corona.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And when you get things put away up there, we've got three PADs and a state vector update for you and a couple more discussion type questions.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: If you want to state vector update, we're in P00, and we can toss you the computer.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We're ready for the update when you're ready.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We're ready with the state vector update when you're ready to Accept.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Our signal strength is going down.
Could we get Omni Charlie for this, please?
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I'm going to roll 180 degrees in just a minute.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Houston, can we get the map update?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I've got a map data - map update for you.
Read back, and I've got the landmark tracking update PAD after that.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Rev-24 map update details are LOS, 121:21:59; sunrise, 121:33:30; crossing 150°W, 121:31:11; AOS, 122:06:09; sunset, 122:45:55.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You ready for your tracking update?
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Charlie Papa 1: 121:39:49, 121:42:49, 3 balls, 050, 3 balls, north 13 miles, 11, 40.
Charlie Papa 2: 121:55:10, 121:56:52, 3 balls, 2 balls 8, 3 balls, north 05, 04, 40.
And I've got a maneuver PAD after that.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Charlie Papa 1: 121:39:49, 121:42:49, all balls, north 13, 11 and 40.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: On Charlie Papa 2, we had 121:55 and 10.
And we want to confirm the nautical miles on Charlie Papa 1 as being 13 - 13.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Charlie Papa 2: 121:55:10, 121:56:52, all balls, 008, all balls, north 05, 04, 40.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Landmark tracking PAD breakdown:Target CP-1T-1:121:39:49 (Initial point when the target is on the local horizon.)T-2:121:42:49 (When the spacecraft is at an elevation angle of 35° above the target.)Roll: 000°, Pitch: 050°, Yaw: 000°Target 13 nautical miles North of the groundtrack.SA-Shaft angle:11°TA-Trunnion angle:40°.Target CP-2T-1:121:55:10T-2:121:56:52Roll: 000°, Pitch: 008°, Yaw: 000°Target 5 nautical miles North of the groundtrack.SA-Shaft angle:4°TA-Trunnion angle:40°.Target F-1T-1:122:07:55T-2:122:10:06Roll: 000°, Pitch: 326°, Yaw: 000°Target 10 nautical miles North of the groundtrack.SA-Shaft angle:9°TA-Trunnion angle:41°.Target 130T-1:122:29:03T-2:122:30:37Roll: 000°, Pitch: 265°, Yaw: 000°Target 13 nautical miles North of the groundtrack.SA-Shaft angle:12°TA-Trunnion angle:40°.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: You got it, and we're ready with the maneuver PAD.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: This is TEI-24: SPS/G&N; 36818; minus 061, plus 076; 123:40:52.33: plus 30443, plus 00131, plus 00682; NA, 066, and the rest is NA.
Under ullage, two jets for l4 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Jack, we had an antenna switch right in the middle of that.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Before we proceed, lets lock up with the High Gain.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We're still maneuvering, Stand by one.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're in Accept and P00 and ready for your update and ready for your PAD.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Reading you loud and clear now.
The uplink's coming up and here's the PAD.
TEI-24: SPS/G&N; 36818; minus 061, plus 076; 123:40:52.33; 0 - correction - plus 30443, plus 00131, plus 00682; roll is NA, pitch is 066.
Your ullage is two jets for 14 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I got TEI-24: SPS/G&N; 36818; minus 061, plus 076; 123:40:52.33; plus 30443, plus 00131, plus 00682; NA, and pitch is 066.
The rest is NA; two jets for 14 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The PAD is interpreted as follows:Purpose:The PAD is a revised version for an emergency burn to return to Earth at the end of Rev 24.Systems:The burn would be made using the SPS engine, under the control of the Guidance and Navigation system.Vehicle weight (Noun 47):36,818 lbs (16,700 kg)Pitch and yaw trim (Noun 48):-0.61° and +0.76°.Time of ignition (Noun 33):123 hours, 40 minutes, 52.33 seconds.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):x, +3044.3 (927.9); y, +0013.1 (4.0); z,+0068.2 (20.8).

The large positive number in the X direction implies a large prograde component, essentially adding to their orbital velocity, exactly what would be expected from an escape manoeuvre.Spacecraft attitude (IMU angles):Roll, NA; Pitch, 6.6°.Two RCS jet ullage for 14 seconds.All the subsequent items on the form are not applicable to this manoeuvre.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Computer is yours; you can go to Block.
And we've noted that on the last pass during the strip photography, you were rolled 180 degrees different than that we expected you to be.
And we'd like to ask you a couple of questions about the LM S-band yesterday and LM pressurization when you have an opportunity to discuss it.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: On the S-band communications around the DOI period - Do you have anything significant to report having lost comm for about 20 minutes on the High Gain there?
We were a little concerned as to what the problem might be.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I noticed we were having, locked - out lockout problems as we went low across the landing site.

It appeared that it occurred right at our low time, and the S-band didn't track - didn't follow us when we came across the landing site, and I went to Omnis hoping without having too much time to play with it.
And, then, a period of time after that, I played with the S-band again and was able to acquire you and lock on.
That's all I can really say, but it did occur somewhere near the low part of our trajectory.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We understand another question is regarding the LM pressurization.
We noted that right after you pickled old Snoopy off Charlie there, that the LM cabin pressurization went down.
Do you - did you observe anything or note anything unusual about that.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Jack, he moved away with a blast, and the next thing we had in our eyes was sunlight right through the windows and we couldn't see a thing.
I do know that both dump valves were in Auto, however.
We had a lot of garbage around after the blast from the pyros, but other than that - Tom may have seen something else.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Well, I was looking out the center hatch window and, as you know, the tunnel - We couldn't get the tunnel depressurized, and when we fired those pyros, some more insulation blew out, and I just saw Snoopy disappear in a big snowstorm going straight up into the Sun and that was all.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: MCC-H are anxious to establish what happened at LM ascent stage separation as it could impact on this mission and the next, Apollo 11.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: When the LM ascent stage was jettisoned, the cabin pressure dropped rapidly.
At the moment of jettison, the pressure was 4.86 psia.
Telemetry from the LM was lost for the next twelve seconds.
Once the telemetry returned the cabin pressure was down to 0.70 psia and it continued to decay slowly.
The jettison of the LM was filmed from the CSM using 16mm magazine Y.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Using the 16mm film taken of the jettison, a history of the relative distance between the two spacecraft was produced.
The maximum acceleration was 50 fps (15.24 m/s) at the moment of jettison.
This had dropped to 4.0 fps (1.22 m/s) after 0.25 seconds.
The acceleration then picked up to a maximum of 8.9 fps (2.71 m/s) at 3.2 seconds after jettison.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The initial acceleration is well in excess of the acceleration imparted by the pyrotechnic separation process.

Due to the fact that the docking tunnel could not be vented, the tunnel remaining pressurized at 4.86 psia when combined with the over-pressure resulting from the pyrotechnic firing was sufficient to cause the LM upper hatch latch to fail (see diagram below).
The latch failure allowed the LM upper hatch to open immediately and then partially close after 0.3 seconds, allowing rapid depressurisation of the LM cabin.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: And last time we saw Snoopy down here, he was 37,000 miles [68,500 km] going straight up from the Moon at 5,400 feet per second [1,645 m/s], and thank you for your comments.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I guess I got run ahead.
We made some changes in here on that roll.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And the way that the times have changed.
But it didn't - I'm sorry about that - but it didn't seem to - We didn't get any shafting or anything on our windows at all until right at the last when we hit the terminator.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: That was the only thing we were concerned about.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I don't think it'll - No, I just don't.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: There was no shafting at all on the windows and it looked like - We were giving our comments and I don't think we had any problem at all.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And understand we will have this attitude for Rev-31.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We'd like you to check the situation with fuel cell 1; ensure that your fuel cell pump's AC circuit breaker on panel 226 is Closed.
And that your fuel cell number 1 AC 1 is Closed - correction - is AC1.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I tried to push in breaker A and it's out, and as soon as we did we got an AC bus 1 light, a main bus A and a main bus B undervolt light.
The breaker will not reset at this time.
and it's fuel cell 1, AC pump breaker on 226.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The AC pump package in fuel cell 1 suffered a short circuit at 121:47.
This caused the associated FC AC PUMP circuit breaker on panel 226 to trip.
The crew attempted to reset this circuit breaker, without success.
Following further analysis of the problem, fuel cell 1 was taken off the bus, open circuited, because both the hydrogen and glycol coolant pumps were now inoperative.
As temperature control of fuel cell 1 had now been lost, it was only put back on the bus for SPS manoeuvres as long as the skin temperature was below 370°F.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The failure was probably a phase to phase short in the hydrogen pump, caused by a breakdown of the wiring insulation due to the passage of hot moist hydrogen across the cables.
The hydrogen pump provides continuous circulation of the hydrogen in the fuel cell primary coolant loop, and withdraws water vapour and heat from the stack of cells.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: With two remaining healthy fuel cells the mission is still able to proceed as planned, whilst monitoring the health of the functioning two fuel cells very closely.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: When I push it in, I get those three lights, AC bus 1, main bus A, and main bus B undervolts.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I just wanted to tell you.
Of course, I got - There goes the fuel cell 1 light.
I think it's probably because of a condenser exhaust temperature down around 154 degrees [Fahrenheit, 68°C].
Also the skin temperature is going up at this time, and we do have the fuel cell 1 light.
The bus lights now are all reset and everything's normal from that.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Cernan is monitoring the fuel cell skin temp using the meter on panel 3.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Here's what we'd like you to do on fuel cell 1.
Fuel cell 2 go to main bus A only.
Fuel cell 3 go to main bus B only.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: You want me to open circuit fuel cell number 1.
You want me to go fuel 2 to main bus A and fuel cell 3 to main bus B.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: In the event that you get an under voltage light, disregard the voltage and come right back up again.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I'm looking at 26.9 [volts] on both main A and main B at this time.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: How good does all that look to you?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: How good does all that really look to you?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: I guess we're going to lose you in a couple of minutes here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Do you want me to maintain my battery charge on A at this time?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Terminate your battery A charge at this time and if we get a Cryo O2heater cycle, well, you may lower the bus voltage momentarily.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I'm terminating the battery A charge at this time.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Houston, do you have a - What's your latest hack on when we'll have AOS, please?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And we'l1 be losing you in 2 minutes.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: 122:06 and we're going to plan to continue on with the landmark tracking, and we'll talk to you about this as soon as we get AOS.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We'll have some new word for you then.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I'm going to go ahead and start a roll around so I'll come around to a Orb Rate of 3 - I'll wait until after we get Loss Of Signal and pick up an Orb Rate of 339 for landmark tracking.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Stafford is encouraging his crewmates to look for any electrical items they could power down to give a margin on the loads being drawn through the electrical buses now they are only drawing current from two fuel cells.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We've had Loss Of Signal from Apollo 10 as it went around behind the Moon on the 23rd revolution.
Our next Acquisition Of Signal will be at 122 hours, 6 minutes Ground Elapsed Time, some 5 minutes and 40 seconds from now.

Apparently one of the 3 Apollo fuel cells is acting up somewhat, and by shifting the switch positions in the spacecraft powering the main busses and the power distribution system they are getting around this slight anomaly.
On the next revolution around the Moon they will be conducting some orbital navigation exercises, landmark tracking.
And at 121 hours, 21 minutes Ground Elapsed Time, this is Apollo Control.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: The fuel cell heater switch on panel 3, allows the automatic operation of the heater to maintain the fuel cell powerplant temperature at 196°C (385°F).
Cernan is anxious to get the heaters off to reduce the load on the remaining two fuel cells, and monitor the fuel cell skin temperature manually, bringing the heaters on when required.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan has switched off the heaters in the H2and O2tanks to save on power.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Cernan is also suggesting powering off the High Gain Antenna whilst the CSM is out of contact with MCC-H, again to save power.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The actual SPS ignition time for TEI at the end of rev 24 is 123:40:52.33 GET as given in the last PAD.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford is referring to the caution and warning lights that are illuminated.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Young is convinced MCC-H will decide to perform TEI early due to the issues with fuel cell 1, therefore curtailing the opportunity for landmark tracking.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford is expressing his concern that MCC-H may decide to perform the Trans Earth Injection (TEI) early due to the problem with fuel cell 1.
The mission rules do not call for an early return.

So long as the remaining two cells remain healthy, and the faulty cell is only open circuited so it can be brought back on line at times of maximum loads, the mission should continue close to the nominal Flight Plan.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Verb 82 is used to display the orbit parameters, whereas Verb 83 displays rendezvous parameters which are no longer required at this stage in the mission.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Having taken the cryo tank heaters off to reduce power load, it is necessary to manually bring them back on line occasionally to maintain the pressure.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Crater 225 is today known as Coriolis.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Basin IX, today known as Mendeleev.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The crew continue to discuss whether they would be better off performing TEI early due to the fuel cell 1 issues.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is Apollo Control at 122 hours, 5 minutes Ground Elapsed Time.
Less than a minute away from acquisition here, as Apollo 10 comes around from behind the Moon on revolution 24.
This next Loss Of Signal will be some hour and 12 minutes from now at 123 hours and 18 minutes.
We're standing by for a resumption of communications here as the spacecraft comes around on this rev.
The crew, at the present time, is tracking various lunar landmarks and lunar orbital navigation experiments.
We should have Acquisition Of Signal at the present time.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Standing by here for the first call from the spacecraft or from the ground to Apollo 10.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: Based on comparisons with contemporary weather satellite images, journal contributor Paul White has determined that the following Earthrise images were taken at about this time.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: We're right square in the middle of a landmark tracking.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And we've cranked up a plan-of-attack on that fuel cell.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Can you hold off for just a couple minutes?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: The crew is still rather busy in the landmark tracking exercise and a few minutes ago asked Spacecraft Communicator Jack Lousma for a few more minutes to complete this particular run before they picked up with the discussion.
We will leave the circuit up, though, and pick up the conversation when it does resume.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We're between the F-1 and 130 sites.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Here is our plan-of-attack on this fuel cell.
First, we're going to look at the fuel cell temperature for a little while, and after we've done that, we're going to put it back on line to look at the temperatures it generates versus its loading.
And then we're going to work up a fuel cell purge.
We've got 25 hours of hydrogen purging available.
Looks like we've lost a pump package in that fuel cell 1, but we're going to maintain the temperatures of the fuel cell by purging it, and then we'll use the cell only for burns, et cetera.

At the present time, we're not proposing any changes in the Flight Plan, and we expect when it goes back on line it will go on both main A and main B, and so at this time we're working up a purge cycle and looking at your fuel cell.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We did - when the heater cycled, we were looking at about 20 to 20.2 volts and we've got the main bus voltage undervolts light.
I've been cycling the heaters to the Auto position on the Cryos, one at a time, as they appeared to need it.
It's just not a good feeling to have those lights - undervolts lights come on in here.
I've also temporarily turned the power on the High Gain Antenna, Off, until we can catch a High Gain again.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: And we've also turned - We've also turned the potable H2O heater, Off.
Just some other small things to start saving power.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: High Gain Antenna, and Potable Water Heater, Off.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: If you see my Cryo pressure dropping, don't hesitate to tell me to get the heater On.
I just don't like to put all four of them on.
We split the load when we get the lights on.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Cernan only wants to use the heaters in one Cryo tank at a time to maintain pressures in the Cryo tanks, but keeping the electrical load to a minimum.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Understand you're cycling your Cryo heaters, and we'll watch the temperatures.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: This is Apollo Control here; 53 minutes remaining until Loss Of Signal on this 24th lunar revolution; continuing to stand by for resumption of communications between Apollo 10 and the ground.
transcript.speaker: PAO

tokens: We have ginned up a fuel cell 1 plan.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Do you have the data off 130 yet?
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We've got the data off 130 now.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: When we're at real time, I'll just hold on.
I understand this is the one that's really important, so I can hang on to this until you give me a Go.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: This is 10, with your fuel cell plan.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Fuel cell plan is relatively simple.
We're just going to leave it off line, open circuit, and we want you to turn the fuel cell 1 inline heaters off and then monitor the skin temperature.
Monitor the temperature to stay between 390 and 410 [°F, 199° and 210°C], cycling the inline heaters on and off to maintain 390 to 410 [°F, 199° to 210°C].
This will keep our water production to a minimum, reducing our requirement to purge, and we may be able to go as long as 50 hours in this manner without purging.
During the day, we will work out procedures to use during your sleep period on skin temperature.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: You must have been reading the same malfunction procedures I was.
The fuel cell heater has been off now ever since we went through LOS.
I've got a question on the heater.
It's an Auto heater, which recycles somewhere around 380, 390 degrees [°F, 193°, 199°C].
Do you just want me to turn it to Auto position if it starts dropping, is that correct?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Or am I wrong about that heater?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We want you to manually keep the temperature between 390 and 410 [°F, 199° and 210°C] by cycling the heater switch.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: What are you reading on the heater - the skin temperature right now?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Right now, we're reading skin temperature of 423 [°F, 217°C], 10.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I'm reading about 430 [°F, 221°C], I guess, and it's been pretty stable.
I'll turn the heater On, say down around 390 [°F, 199°C], and keep it between 390 and 410 [°F, 199° and 210°C].
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger, And whenever you've got some time there, we would like to update your state vector and pass you some PADs.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I'm ready to copy your PAD, and I guess I've got another question.
You might be thinking up some words and things we can pull off the line here pretty quick in case we do get some under-volting problems going behind the backside, and I'm ready to copy your PAD.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We're working on that and we will give you the word.
I have a map update PAD, Rev-25: 123:18:32, 123:29...
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I thought you meant a PAD.
We've got to get the right book out here.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Roger, I have a maneuver PAD.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Fixing to do a map update.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: Want you to check the AOS to be 124:04:21; sunset, 124:44:39.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Rev 25 map update details are LOS, 123:18:32; sunrise, 123:29:36; crossing 150°W, 123:32:14; AOS, 124:04:21; sunset, 124:44:39
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: I've got a landmark tracking PAD.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Charlie Papa 1: 123:38:14, 123:41:13, three balls, 051, three balls, north 13 miles, 12, 41.
Foxtrot 1: 124:06:20, 124:08:30, three balls, 329, three balls, north 10, 10, 40.
Landmark 130: 124:27:28, 124:29:00, three balls, 265, three balls, north at 12, 12, 41.
Give me a readback and tell me when you are ready for a maneuver PAD.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Landmark tracking PAD:Target CP-1T-1:123:38:14 (Initial point when the target is on the local horizon.)T-2:123:41:13 (When the spacecraft is at an elevation angle of 35° above the target.)Roll: 000°, Pitch: 051°, Yaw: 000°Target 13 nautical miles (24km) north of the groundtrack.SA-Shaft angle:12°TA-Trunnion angle:41°.Target CP-2T-1:123:53:35T-2:123:55:17Roll: 000°, Pitch: 007°, Yaw: 000°Target 5 nautical miles (9.3 km) north of the groundtrack.SA-Shaft angle:4°TA-Trunnion angle:42°.Target F-1T-1:124:06:20T-2:124:08:30Roll: 000°, Pitch: 329°, Yaw: 000°Target 10 nautical miles (18.5 km) north of the groundtrack.SA-Shaft angle:10°TA-Trunnion angle:40°.Target 130T-1:124:27:28T-2:124:29:00Roll: 000°, Pitch: 265°, Yaw: 000°Target 12 nautical miles (22 km) north of the groundtrack.SA-Shaft angle:12°TA-Trunnion angle:41°.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: This is TEI number 25, SPS/G&N: 36750, minus 0.61, plus 0.75; 125:40:03.81.
plus 3100.8, plus 0011.2, plus 0115.4; roll is NA, pitch is 064, and the rest is NA.
Your ullage is two jets for 14 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Roll is NA, pitch is 064, and two jets for 14 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: The PAD is interpreted as follows:Purpose:The PAD is a revised version for an emergency burn to return to Earth at the end of Rev 25.Systems:The burn would be made using the SPS engine, under the control of the Guidance and Navigation system.Vehicle weight (Noun 47):36,750 lbs (16,670 kg)Pitch and yaw trim (Noun 48):-0.61° and +0.75°.Time of ignition (Noun 33):125 hours, 40 minutes, 03.81 seconds.Change in velocity (Noun 81), fps (m/s):X, +3100.8 (945.1); Y, +0011.2 (3.4); Z, +0115.4 (35.2).

The large positive number in the X direction implies a large prograde component, essentially adding to their orbital velocity, exactly what would be expected from an escape manoeuvre.Spacecraft attitude (IMU angles):Roll, NA; Pitch, 6.4°.Additional notes are that subsequent items on the form are not applicable to this manoeuvre, and that two RCS jets will be used to settle propellants, firing for 14 seconds.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: That's a good readback, and we're finished with our uplink.
The computer is yours; go to Block.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: That F-l was right near the subsolar point, and boy I really had a lot of trouble trying to figure out transfer.
You could see it okay in the telescope but when you transfer from the telescope to the sextant it just vanishes.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: CP-2 is sort of that way, too.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I'm going to pitch around and go to 092, inertial.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: It's there, but you just can't see it.

It's got the landmark in there, landmark line of sight and the lunar line of sight in there, all in one in the sextant, and you've got two different images and they're so bright it just doesn't - unless it has the dark feature in it, which most of these places don't.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Understand it's hard to see because it's bright as opposed to its size.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I just don't get any definition.
It's a bright blob down there, and there's whole bunch of bright blobs down there.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: John, wherever you have a target that looks too bright in the sextant, we just recommend finding it in the telescope and using it that way.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We're just squared away down to a better IMU align attitude.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: And I'd like to tell John that he can use the telescope where necessary, if field of view in the sextant is too bright.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: 130 is no problem; that's easy to get, so I used the sextant on it.
But maybe on F-1 and CP-2, I'll use the telescope.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: Go ahead and use that telescope if it's too bright to get in the sextant.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Whoever thought of using the telescope on landmark tracking.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I was just making a facetious remark.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: And they'll buy the telescope data.
And we'd like to have the High Gain Antenna for a dump.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Could you give me some High Gain angles, please, for my attitude?
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: If you haven't found them already, it's pitch minus 70, yaw 192.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: I'll be with you in a minute.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Houston is reading you loud and clear.
We're going to start the dump.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: And your LOS will be at 123:18, about 17 minutes.
And we owe you a powerdown list.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Will you have it by then?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: If we don't, we'll find out why.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We have some information on potential powerdown items.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: If you don't want to interfere with tracking and photography operations, there's not much more that you can power down that you've not already powered down.
However, if you elect to terminate your tracking and photography operations, refer to checklist page S2-8 which is powerdown, SPS burn.
Start at the top and start powering down those items.
However, do not power down Batt C.
Delete Batt C on Main A and B from the checklist and delete fuel cell pumps, 3, Off.
Delete those two items and use that powerdown checklist as your guide.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Yes, I was looking at that.

I guess what I was looking for was some words on - not on emergency powerdown but, you know, in case this Undervolt comes on and persists to stay on, there might be a few other little things that we've overlooked, like I'm playing these heaters manually.
We turned the portable heater off and turn the S-band off when we lose you, and a few of those things.
I wasn't looking for the emergency conditions, just maybe a few good words or something that would be very obvious to you and not to us.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: You've already done a pretty good job of figuring that out, Gene.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Just keep us informed, because this landmark tracking is real important.
We'll get this bear wired down and we've already got one set, and we're going to continue on here and we'll monitor this cell.
And, naturally, if the thing really starts to go out on us, we know what the mission rule is on it.
But right now, we plan to continue on and primarily concentrate on the landmark tracking.
We've shot so much photography we're about out of color film.
We're saving a little bit for the way back.
And we still have some black and white to go, and we'll do some of that, but the main thing we're going to concentrate on now is the landmark tracking.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: We see no reason to change your plan of attack, and we have a change to landmark tracking update PAD Foxtrot 1.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Stand by and I'll copy it.
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: On Rev-25, target Foxtrot 1, the T2time should be changed.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: And so far, all the tracking data looks real good.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: You got a pretty good way to evaluate it in real time, there?
transcript.speaker: Stafford

tokens: I didn't get all the marks on one of those places, because - because I lost it in the sextant.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: I forget, It's one of the CPs back there.
transcript.speaker: Young

tokens: We detected that on CP-2, but 130 is the real important one, and that's coming through real good.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: We have a revision to the way in which we'd like you to maintain temperature in the Cryo tanks.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Instead of using your heaters to maintain the temperature in the Cryo tanks, use your fans manually.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: Use the fans to maintain the pressure in the Cryo tanks.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Use the fans to maintain the temperature in the Cryo tanks.
This will require less current and allow finer control.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: MMC-H is suggesting using the cryo tank fans to maintain the pressure rather than using the tank heaters.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Those are the kind of good words I really wanted.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: We'll try to think up some more.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: You're going around the corner in about a minute and a half.
Your AOS is 124:04, and we'd like you to check and make sure the VHF is all Off.
transcript.speaker: Lousma

tokens: All the VHF switches are all Off.
transcript.speaker: Cernan

tokens: Young is probably referring to the ECS Radiator switches on panel 2.
They select or disable the Primary Heater Control No.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: Stafford and Young are not very complimentary of the apparent drift rate of the Gyro Display Coupler (GDC).
The GDC takes signals from the BMAGs which indicate the rate of the spacecraft's rotation.
Given a known starting attitude, the GDC can produce attitude angles from those rate signals and make them available to drive the FDAI displays.
Since the BMAG gyros' rate signals are never very accurate, the GDC's idea of attitude appears to drift substantially compared to the IMU.
However, it is straightforward for the crew to give the GDC a fresh update of true attitude by pressing GDC Align.
This applies IMU-derived attitude angles to the GDC and therefore gives it a known starting point for its processing.
transcript.speaker: Commentary

tokens: The Exer-Genie is a bungee type exerciser.
transcript.speaker: Commentary
